title: Chapter 3 — Ethics and Privacy in Data Work
objectives: Distinguish ethics from privacy and map stakeholders across the dataset lifecycle
Apply core ethical principles as operational design tests, not post-hoc slogans
Implement consent, minimization, and Privacy by Design as privacy-management practice
Compare anonymization, encryption, differential privacy, and federated learning
Recognize selection and algorithmic bias and connect ethics overview to Chapter 7 depth
Navigate GDPR, HIPAA, and CCPA as planning maps for data teams
Analyze breach failure modes, consequences, and prevention controls
Use utilitarian, rights-based, virtue, and justice lenses for ethical decisions
Anticipate AI-scale surveillance, fragmented regulation, and emerging privacy tools

parts.title: Introduction to ethics and privacy
parts.bridge_in: Chapter 2 asked how records enter a dataset; this chapter asks what obligations attach when those records concern people. This first part defines ethics and privacy, contrasts their focus, and shows why ethical data work is a coordination problem across roles—not a checklist owned by one engineer.

parts.title: Core ethical principles
parts.bridge_in: With ethics and privacy distinguished, the next step is to turn principles into tests you can apply during design. This part walks five recurring principles—non-maleficence, fairness, transparency, accountability, and autonomy—with short scenarios from healthcare, hiring, and health apps.
parts.split_reason: Section 3.2 is too long for one clip; core principles (sec:3.2.1) are covered here; utility, dilemmas, and consequences follow in part-03.

parts.title: Utility, dilemmas, and consequences
parts.bridge_in: Principles name what good practice should respect; real projects still face trade-offs when utility and privacy pull in opposite directions. This part covers the utility–privacy tension, ethical dilemmas when principles conflict, and the harms that follow when ethics is neglected.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:3.2 after core principles (sec:3.2.2–3.2.4).

parts.title: Data privacy principles
parts.bridge_in: Ethical principles set the bar; privacy principles show how to implement individual control in a pipeline. This part operationalizes informed consent, anonymization, and Privacy by Design, then previews GDPR and HIPAA as enforceable anchors.

parts.title: Privacy-preserving techniques
parts.bridge_in: Consent and design defaults matter, but teams also need technical controls when data must be shared or analyzed at scale. This part compares anonymization, encryption, differential privacy, and federated learning—and what each protects.

parts.title: Bias and fairness
parts.bridge_in: Privacy controls limit exposure; fairness asks whether outcomes systematically disadvantage groups once models deploy. This part introduces bias in data work, selection versus algorithmic bias, landmark case pointers, and a mitigation checklist—Chapter 7 develops metrics and tools in depth.

parts.title: Real-world privacy laws
parts.bridge_in: Principles and techniques guide design; statutes set enforceable floors. This part surveys GDPR, HIPAA, CCPA/CPRA, and selected other laws as planning maps for scope, rights, and penalties—not as a substitute for counsel.

parts.title: Data breaches
parts.bridge_in: Laws assume teams can detect and report incidents; breaches show what happens when controls fail. This part defines breaches, lists recurring causes, and walks Equifax, Yahoo, and Target as three failure-mode patterns.

parts.title: Breach consequences and prevention
parts.bridge_in: Knowing how breaches start is only half the story; organizations also need to understand financial, reputational, and regulatory fallout. This part ties costs to the case studies from the previous part and maps prevention controls to each failure mode.

parts.title: Ethical decision-making
parts.bridge_in: Controls and statutes reduce risk, but teams still choose among imperfect options when principles conflict. This part introduces utilitarian, rights-based, virtue, and justice lenses, applies them to marketing collection and criminal-justice scoring, and stresses stakeholder involvement before launch.

parts.title: Emerging trends and challenges
parts.bridge_in: Decision frameworks help today’s dilemmas; AI scale and fragmented global rules will keep generating new ones. This final part covers surveillance and prediction risks, accountability debates, cross-border regulation friction, and near-term directions including privacy-enhancing technologies and self-sovereign identity.
